solution.p: We will factor y^{2}-14y+49 where a=1, b=-14, and c=49.

Perfect Square Formula: The formula for perfect square trinomials is (a \pm b)^2=a^2 \pm 2ab + b^2. Note that the polynomial y^{2}-14y+49 matches the formula's right side, and we have: \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}-14y+49\amp =\left(y\right)^2-2\left(y\right)\left(7\right)+\left(7\right)^2\newline \amp =\left(y-7\right)\mathopen{}\left(y-7\right)\newline \amp =\left(y-7\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.

For y^{2}-14y+49, since the product of -7 and -7 is 49, and the sum of -7 and -7 is -14, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}-14y+49\amp =\left(y-7\right)\mathopen{}\left(y-7\right)\newline \amp =\left(y-7\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (49) = 49.

The number 49 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(49)\amp -1(-49)\amp 7(7)\amp -7(-7)
ewline\end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of -14, and this sum happens with (-7)+(-7)=-14 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]y^{2}-14y+49\amp =y^{2}-7y-7y+49
ewline \amp =y\mathopen{}\left(y-7ight)-7\mathopen{}\left(y-7ight)
ewline \amp =\left(y-7ight)\mathopen{}\left(y-7ight)
ewline \amp =\left(y-7ight)^{2}\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in y^{2}-14y+49 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}
The third term, 49, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(49)\amp -1(-49)\amp 7(7)\amp -7(-7)\newline\end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with y^{2}-14y+49 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:

Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}-14y+49\amp =\left(y-7\right)\mathopen{}\left(y-7\right)\newline \amp =\left(y-7\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
